rationale.R1.2: The paper frames the system as a model 'learning when to invoke external tools' with 'self-supervised tool-selection,' but the described mechanism is standard WFST-constrained beam search with CMUdict — a classical pipeline component, not a learned tool-selection policy. Checking whether this argument is internally coherent is central to any 'what do you think' response.
rationale.R1.3: The WER gains (2.8% test-clean, 8.1→5.9% rare-word, 14.2→9.7% medical) are plausible, but the improvement is attributable to lexicon-constrained decoding rather than any self-supervised tool invocation mechanism. Whether the evidence actually supports the paper's specific claims about tool learning versus traditional FST integration is load-bearing.
rationale.R1.4: CTC + WFST lexicon decoding with CMUdict augmentation is well-established (Graves 2006 onward; standard Kaldi/ESPnet pipelines). Whether this constitutes a novel contribution versus relabeling known methods under 'tool-augmented ASR' framing is a plausible primary concern warranting scrutiny.

parsed.clarification_question: Is your primary concern whether the paper's WER results actually support its claim that the model learns self-supervised tool invocation (evidence-claim alignment, R1.3), or whether the approach is genuinely novel versus standard WFST-constrained CTC decoding (novelty assessment, R1.4)? These lead to materially different critiques.
